content.text: A community cloud is a cloud deployment model in which infrastructure is shared exclusively among a defined group of organizations with common concerns — typically a shared compliance regime, regulatory jurisdiction, security classification level, or mission. Access is restricted to vetted members of that community; the general public cannot sign up. AWS implements this model through purpose-built regions and programs: AWS GovCloud (US) for US government and defense, AWS China regions for China-domiciled entities, classified cloud regions for intelligence and defense, and Dedicated Local Zones for specific geographic communities.
Defining Characteristics of a Community Cloud
A deployment qualifies as a community cloud when it meets these criteria:
Restricted membership.

Only organizations that meet eligibility requirements can access the environment. Eligibility is based on criteria such as US personhood (GovCloud), ICP licensure (China), holding a specific security clearance, or membership in a regulated industry body.
Shared compliance posture.

The environment is certified against a compliance framework that all members need — FedRAMP High, DoD Impact Level 5, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, or ITAR — and that compliance is inherited or shared across members rather than each member having to certify their own data center.
Shared cost.
Infrastructure costs are distributed across all community members through a pay-per-use model, making the compliance-grade environment more affordable than each member building a private cloud.
Governance by or for the community.

The provider (AWS) designs the environment to the community's regulatory requirements and may give a governing body (such as the US Department of Defense) visibility or input into security controls, audit reports, and incident response.

Community cloud sits between public and private cloud on the spectrum of isolation and control. It is more accessible (lower per-member cost) than a private cloud, and more compliant and restricted than a public cloud. Members share underlying hardware and operational infrastructure but are logically isolated from each other at the network and IAM layers.

attrs.alt: Community cloud architecture showing government, healthcare, financial, and research organizations connected to a shared restricted AWS infrastructure, with general public denied access
attrs.title: Community Cloud Architecture

content.text: Multiple distinct communities share the physical infrastructure but are isolated from each other. The compliance certifications and access controls are enforced at the region level rather than by each tenant independently.
Community Cloud vs Other Deployment Models
Understanding the trade-offs relative to other models clarifies when community cloud is the right choice.
Dimension
Public Cloud
Community Cloud
Private Cloud
Access
Anyone with a credit card
Vetted community members only
Single organization only
Cost model
Lowest — widest sharing
Medium — shared among fewer members, premium pricing for compliance assurance
Highest — all cost borne by one tenant
Compliance
Broad (SOC2, PCI, HIPAA BAA) but not highest levels
Community-specific (FedRAMP High, DoD IL5, ITAR, CJIS)
Fully customizable — whatever the org implements
Isolation
Logical (VPC, IAM)
Physical + logical (region restricted to community)
Full physical isolation
Service breadth
Full 200+ service catalog
Subset approved for the community
Depends on Outposts / Dedicated Host selection
AWS Community Cloud Offerings
AWS offers four distinct community cloud environments, each targeting a different community and compliance requirement.
AWS GovCloud (US)

AWS GovCloud is an isolated pair of AWS regions (us-gov-west-1 and us-gov-east-1) designed for US government agencies, contractors, and organizations handling US-controlled data. It is the most comprehensive community cloud offering in the AWS portfolio.
Accessible only to US entities (organizations and individuals) — AWS contractually verifies this before granting access.
Holds FedRAMP High, DoD Impact Level 2 through 5, ITAR, CJIS, HIPAA, and IRS-1075 compliance certifications.
AWS operations staff for GovCloud are US citizens located in the United States.
FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic endpoints are available for all supported services.
Most commercial AWS services are available, but some (especially newer services) launch in commercial regions first. The GovCloud service catalog is a subset of commercial AWS.
AWS China Regions

China's cybersecurity law (MLPS 2.0) requires cloud services operating in mainland China to be run by a licensed Chinese entity and to keep data within China's borders. AWS operates two regions in China — cn-north-1 (Beijing, operated by Sinnet) and cn-northwest-1 (Ningxia, operated by NWCD) — as a joint venture under Chinese law.
Requires a separate AWS China account; global AWS accounts cannot access China regions.
Customers must hold a valid ICP (Internet Content Provider) license to serve public internet traffic in China.
Data processed in China regions is subject to Chinese law, including government data-access requirements.
The service catalog is a subset of global AWS; some services (e.g., Route 53 public zones, Amazon Connect) are not available in China.
AWS Secret and Top Secret Regions

AWS operates classified cloud regions for US intelligence and defense agencies that handle National Security information at the Secret and Top Secret classification levels. These are not publicly documented in detail, but their existence and general capabilities have been confirmed by AWS and the US Intelligence Community.
Access requires US government personnel security clearance at the appropriate level.
Physical and logical isolation is at a level beyond GovCloud — facilities and operations staff meet cleared-facility requirements.
AWS won the C2E (Commercial Cloud Enterprise) contract to provide these services to all 17 US intelligence agencies.
AWS Dedicated Local Zones

Dedicated Local Zones are single-tenant AWS infrastructure deployments in a specific location, managed by AWS but available only to a specific customer or group of customers. They provide community cloud characteristics at a geographic level: a state government, a large university system, or a group of hospitals in a metro area can get a dedicated Zone close to their facilities.
Sub-10ms latency to the dedicated community's facilities.
Single tenant on the physical hardware — no other customers share the Zone.
AWS manages the hardware; the customer controls networking and workload placement.
Used by media and entertainment companies needing ultra-low-latency rendering farms, and by government agencies requiring in-state data residency.
AWS GovCloud Deep Dive
GovCloud is the AWS community cloud most practitioners encounter. Understanding how accounts, identity, and services differ from commercial AWS is essential before working in it.

GovCloud accounts are linked to a commercial AWS account (the root account that pays the bill) but are otherwise separate. You must use the GovCloud-specific management console at https://console.amazonaws-us-gov.com and the GovCloud API endpoints, which use the region string
us-gov-west-1
or
us-gov-east-1

# Configure AWS CLI for GovCloud (separate profile from commercial)
aws configure --profile govcloud
# AWS Access Key ID: (GovCloud credentials — not the same as commercial)
# Default region name: us-gov-west-1
# Default output format: json

# Verify you are in GovCloud
aws sts get-caller-identity --profile govcloud
# {
#   "UserId": "AIDAEXAMPLE",
#   "Account": "123456789012",      # GovCloud account ID
#   "Arn": "arn:aws-us-gov:iam::123456789012:user/govcloud-admin"
# }                                  # Note: arn prefix is aws-us-gov, not aws

# Use FIPS endpoint for S3 (required for FedRAMP High and DoD IL5)
aws s3 ls --profile govcloud \
  --endpoint-url https://s3-fips.us-gov-west-1.amazonaws.com

# Create an S3 bucket in GovCloud
aws s3api create-bucket \
  --profile govcloud \
  --bucket my-agency-data-2025 \
  --region us-gov-west-1 \
  --create-bucket-configuration LocationConstraint=us-gov-west-1
Note that ARNs in GovCloud use the partition
aws-us-gov
instead of
aws
. Any hardcoded ARNs, IAM policies, or CloudFormation templates that reference the commercial partition will not work without modification in GovCloud.
Identity and Access in GovCloud

GovCloud IAM is functionally identical to commercial IAM — same policies, roles, and permission model — but the endpoints and ARN partition differ. IAM Identity Center (SSO) is available in GovCloud for centralized access management across multiple GovCloud accounts.
MFA enforcement:
FedRAMP High and DoD IL4+ require multi-factor authentication for all human users. Enforce this with an IAM policy that denies all actions unless the
aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent
condition is true.
PIV / CAC cards:

US government users typically authenticate with Personal Identity Verification (PIV) smart cards. IAM Identity Center can federate with an on-premises Active Directory that requires PIV-based authentication before issuing SAML assertions.
Service Control Policies (SCPs):
Use AWS Organizations with SCPs to prevent GovCloud accounts from having any connectivity to commercial AWS resources, ensuring data stays in the GovCloud partition.
Compliance Certifications in Community Clouds
Each community cloud environment carries a specific set of certifications. The table below maps the major AWS community cloud options to the compliance frameworks they support.
Certification
GovCloud (US)
Commercial AWS
Required Community
FedRAMP High
Yes
FedRAMP Moderate only
Federal agencies (High impact systems)
DoD IL4 / IL5
Yes
DoD IL2 only
Dept. of Defense / military
ITAR
Yes
No
Defense contractors, aerospace
CJIS
Yes
State-level agreements
Law enforcement, criminal justice
HIPAA BAA
Yes
Yes
Healthcare providers / payers
Yes
No
Tax agencies, FTI handlers
Healthcare Community Cloud on AWS (HIPAA)

HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) governs the handling of Protected Health Information (PHI). AWS enables HIPAA-covered entities and business associates to use commercial AWS services for PHI workloads by signing a Business Associate Agreement (BAA), which extends legal responsibility to AWS for data it processes.

Not all AWS services are eligible under a HIPAA BAA. AWS publishes a list of HIPAA-eligible services — currently over 170 services including EC2, S3, RDS, Lambda, ECS, and EKS. PHI must only be stored or processed using services on that list.
Sign the AWS HIPAA BAA through the AWS Artifact console before storing any PHI. The BAA covers the shared-responsibility obligations for HIPAA security rule compliance.
Enable encryption at rest (S3 SSE-KMS, EBS encryption, RDS encryption) and in transit (TLS 1.2+) for all PHI.
Enable CloudTrail in all regions and CloudWatch Logs for all HIPAA-eligible services to maintain audit logs required by the HIPAA Security Rule.
Use VPC with private subnets and no direct internet access for systems storing PHI. Route all internet-bound traffic through a NAT gateway.

# Create a KMS key for PHI encryption (HIPAA requires key management audit)
KEY_ID=$(aws kms create-key \
  --description "PHI encryption key" \
  --key-usage ENCRYPT_DECRYPT \
  --origin AWS_KMS \
  --query 'KeyMetadata.KeyId' --output text)

aws kms create-alias \
  --alias-name alias/phi-key \
  --target-key-id "$KEY_ID"

# Create an S3 bucket with SSE-KMS for PHI storage
aws s3api create-bucket \
  --bucket my-hipaa-phi-2025 \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --create-bucket-configuration LocationConstraint=us-east-1

# Enforce SSE-KMS on all objects
aws s3api put-bucket-encryption \
  --bucket my-hipaa-phi-2025 \
  --server-side-encryption-configuration '{
    "Rules": [{
      "ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault": {
        "SSEAlgorithm": "aws:kms",
        "KMSMasterKeyID": "alias/phi-key"
      },
      "BucketKeyEnabled": true
    }]
  }'

# Block all public access (PHI must never be publicly readable)
aws s3api put-public-access-block \
  --bucket my-hipaa-phi-2025 \
  --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true
Financial Services Community Cloud

Financial services are among the heaviest users of community cloud concepts on AWS. Regulations like PCI-DSS (payment card data), SOX (financial reporting), GLBA (consumer financial data), and DORA (EU digital operational resilience) all require specific controls that AWS supports through its compliance programs, BAAs, and the GovCloud partition for US-specific requirements.
PCI-DSS Level 1:

AWS is a PCI-DSS Level 1 Service Provider. The Cardholder Data Environment (CDE) can run on AWS if the customer implements required controls: network segmentation (isolated VPC for CDE), tokenization, TLS everywhere, WAF on internet-facing services, and quarterly ASV scans.
AWS PrivateLink for financial APIs:

Connect to third-party financial data providers (market data feeds, payment processors) through AWS PrivateLink rather than the public internet, keeping traffic on the AWS backbone and avoiding exposure to internet threats.
Secrets Manager for credential rotation:
Financial regulations require frequent rotation of database credentials and API keys. Secrets Manager automates rotation for RDS, Redshift, and custom Lambda-based rotation functions.

# PCI-DSS: enable CloudTrail with log file validation (required for audit integrity)
aws cloudtrail create-trail \
  --name pci-audit-trail \
  --s3-bucket-name my-pci-cloudtrail-bucket \
  --include-global-service-events \
  --is-multi-region-trail \
  --enable-log-file-validation  # Ensures logs cannot be tampered with

aws cloudtrail start-logging --name pci-audit-trail

# PCI-DSS: enable GuardDuty for continuous threat detection
aws guardduty create-detector --enable --finding-publishing-frequency SIX_HOURS

# PCI-DSS: enable Security Hub with PCI-DSS standard
aws securityhub enable-security-hub
aws securityhub batch-enable-standards \
  --standards-subscription-requests \
  '[{"StandardsArn":"arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::standards/pci-dss/v/3.2.1"}]'
Audit and Compliance Monitoring

Community clouds require ongoing evidence of compliance, not just a one-time certification. AWS provides managed tools for continuous compliance monitoring that generate the audit artifacts required by community frameworks.
AWS Config:
Records every configuration change to AWS resources and evaluates them against managed or custom rules. Rules map directly to compliance controls — e.g.,
s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited
for FedRAMP or HIPAA.
AWS Security Hub:

Aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and AWS Config into a single pane of glass. Built-in security standards for FedRAMP, PCI-DSS, CIS AWS Foundations, and NIST 800-53 provide scored compliance posture continuously.
AWS Artifact:

On-demand access to AWS compliance reports (SOC 1/2/3, FedRAMP authorization letters, ISO certifications, PCI-DSS AOC). Auditors can download these reports directly rather than requiring AWS to produce them manually for each audit.
AWS Audit Manager:
Maps AWS resource configurations and CloudTrail events to specific audit control requirements, automatically gathering evidence for FedRAMP, HIPAA, SOC2, and PCI-DSS audits.

# Enable AWS Config to record all resource changes
aws configservice put-configuration-recorder \
  --configuration-recorder name=default,roleARN=arn:aws-us-gov:iam::111122223333:role/AWSConfigRole \
  --recording-group allSupported=true,includeGlobalResourceTypes=true

# Deploy the FedRAMP Operational Best Practices conformance pack
aws configservice put-conformance-pack \
  --conformance-pack-name FedRAMP-Operational-Best-Practices \
  --template-s3-uri s3://awsconfigconforms/aws-config-conformance-packs/Operational-Best-Practices-for-FedRAMP.yaml \
  --delivery-s3-bucket my-config-delivery-bucket

# Check compliance summary
aws configservice describe-conformance-pack-compliance \
  --conformance-pack-name FedRAMP-Operational-Best-Practices \
  --query 'ConformancePackRuleComplianceList[?ComplianceType==`NON_COMPLIANT`]'
Connecting to a Community Cloud

Community cloud environments typically require private connectivity rather than internet access. AWS Direct Connect with GovCloud provides dedicated private fiber to GovCloud regions; for China, Alibaba Cloud Express Connect or dedicated internet links to the China regions are used.
Direct Connect to GovCloud:
Order a Direct Connect connection at a co-location facility. Create a Private VIF to your GovCloud VPC. The connection and all traffic stays off the public internet — required for DoD IL5.
Site-to-Site VPN as a backup:
Even in community clouds, a VPN backup path is advisable. GovCloud supports Site-to-Site VPN with IKEv2 and AES-256 encryption over IPSec.
Commercial-to-GovCloud link:

AWS allows a GovCloud account to peer with a commercial VPC (for non-sensitive ancillary services like log shipping to a commercial SIEM), but this must be explicitly architected. CUI and classified data must not traverse commercial regions.
Benefits
Compliance-grade infrastructure at shared cost — members inherit certifications (FedRAMP, HIPAA, PCI) rather than building them independently.
Faster time to authority to operate (ATO) for federal agencies because the underlying platform is pre-authorized.
Access to AWS cloud scale and managed services within the compliant boundary — no need to rebuild everything in a dedicated on-premises stack.
Physical and logical isolation from the general public, reducing the surface area for social-engineering or supply-chain attacks from non-community entities.
Consistent operational tooling (AWS Console, CLI, CloudFormation, Terraform) that security teams can train on once and apply across commercial and community environments.
Trade-offs
Community clouds introduce constraints that commercial AWS users do not face.
Smaller service catalog — GovCloud and China regions lag behind commercial AWS in service availability. New AWS services typically launch in commercial regions first, sometimes by 6–18 months.
Higher pricing — community cloud regions carry a compliance premium. GovCloud pricing is typically 10–15% higher than equivalent commercial regions.
Separate account management — GovCloud and China accounts are not part of the commercial AWS account hierarchy, requiring separate billing, IAM, and organizational management.

Migration friction — ARN partition differences, endpoint URL differences, and service gaps mean commercial-to-GovCloud migrations require more engineering effort than commercial region-to-region moves.
Eligibility verification adds onboarding delay — GovCloud accounts can take days to weeks to provision while AWS verifies US-person eligibility.
When to Choose Community Cloud
Your workload handles data that is legally required to remain within a specific jurisdiction or to be processed only by personnel with specific clearances (ITAR, CJIS, FedRAMP High, DoD IL4/IL5).
You need to obtain a FedRAMP authorization to operate and your system operates above the Moderate baseline.
Your customers (government agencies, healthcare providers, financial institutions) contractually require their data to be processed in a compliant environment with specific AWS certifications.
You are serving the Chinese market and must comply with MLPS 2.0 data residency requirements, mandating a China-region account.
A private cloud's compliance posture is required, but building and certifying dedicated infrastructure is not economically justified — community cloud provides that posture at shared cost.
Key Takeaways

Community cloud shares infrastructure among a restricted group of organizations with common compliance, regulatory, or mission requirements — accessible only to vetted members, not the general public.

AWS offers four community cloud implementations: GovCloud (US) for US government and defense, China regions for MLPS-compliant workloads, classified regions for intelligence agencies, and Dedicated Local Zones for specific geographic communities.

AWS GovCloud provides the most comprehensive community cloud on AWS, with FedRAMP High, DoD IL2–5, ITAR, CJIS, HIPAA, and IRS-1075 certifications, FIPS 140-2 endpoints, and US-citizen-only AWS operations staff.

Key trade-offs include smaller service catalogs (new services arrive later), higher pricing, separate account structures, and ARN partition differences that require code adjustments when migrating from commercial AWS.

Continuous compliance monitoring (AWS Config conformance packs, Security Hub compliance standards, AWS Audit Manager, and AWS Artifact) is as important as the initial certification — community clouds require ongoing evidence of compliance.
